sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “Apart from You I Have No Good Thing”

heb.paragraph: A miktam of David. The term appears in six psalm titles (16, 56–60). Its meaning is uncertain — possibly "inscription" or "golden poem." Psalm 16 is the most important because of its resurrection content.

refs.note: "I consider everything a loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ" — Paul’s version of "apart from you I have no good thing."
"The LORD is my portion; therefore I will wait for him" — the identical ḥēleq theology.

notes.note: "Apart from you I have no good thing." Total dependence on God as the source of all good.
"LORD, you are my portion and my cup." The inheritance language: other tribes received land; Levi received God (Num 18:20). David claims the Levitical privilege: God himself is enough.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "I have no good apart from you" is either "my good is not beyond you" or "you are my only good." Either reading: total sufficiency in God. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: the portion theology is the heart of contentment. Calvin: whoever has God has everything; whoever lacks God has nothing, regardless of possessions. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥēleq (portion) + kōs (cup) + gōrāl (lot) — three inheritance terms stacked. The emphasis is unmistakable: God is David’s entire inheritance. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "my portion" is Levitical language. The Levites received no land inheritance; God was their inheritance. David claims this for himself — God above all possessions. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the psalm’s first half establishes exclusive devotion. Only God is David’s source. This prepares for the resurrection declaration in the second half. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "you make my lot secure" — the lot-casting language of land distribution (Josh 14–19). David’s lot is not territory but the LORD himself. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David takes refuge in God (v.1), declares that apart from God he has no good (v.2), delights in the saints (v.3), and rejects other gods (v.4). "LORD, you alone are my portion and my cup; you make my lot secure" (v.5). The ḥēleq (portion) language echoes Ecclesiastes: God is the portion that death cannot take.

sections.header: Verses 7–11 — “You Will Not Abandon Me to the Realm of the Dead”

heb.gloss: Sheol / the grave / the realm of the dead
heb.paragraph: "You will not abandon me to the realm of the dead (shəʾōl), nor will you let your faithful one see decay" (v.10). The resurrection verse. Peter quotes this at Pentecost (Acts 2:25–28): David’s body DID see decay, so the psalm prophesies Christ’s resurrection. Paul repeats the argument (Acts 13:35–37). This is one of the OT’s clearest resurrection texts.

"You make known to me the path (nətîb) of life" (v.11). The "path of life" leads through death to resurrection. Not avoidance of death but passage through it. "You will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand." Joy, presence, eternity — the psalm’s final three words describe heaven.

refs.note: Peter quotes Psalm 16:8–11 at Pentecost: "You will not let your Holy One see decay" — applied to Christ’s resurrection.
Paul quotes Psalm 16:10 in Antioch: David died and decayed; Jesus did not. The psalm is prophetic.
"Death has been swallowed up in victory" — the resurrection the psalm anticipates.

notes.note: "My body also will rest secure, because you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead." The resurrection hope: not mere survival but bodily preservation through death.
"In your presence there is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore." The psalm’s climax: eternal joy in God’s presence. Not temporary comfort but everlasting pleasure.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "let your faithful one see the pit" — shaḥat (pit/decay) is the grave’s consuming reality. The psalm promises exemption from corruption’s final word. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: David speaks beyond himself. Calvin: the Spirit inspired words that David’s own experience could not fulfil. The psalm’s true subject is the Son who conquered death.
Calvin: "fullness of joy" — not partial, not mixed, not temporary. Full, unmixed, eternal. This is the beatific vision promised in Psalm 11:7, now linked to resurrection.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the key interpretive question: does "faithful one" (hasîdək̅ā) refer to David or to a future figure? The NT’s consistent answer: ultimately Christ.
The NET: "pleasures" (nəʿîmōṭ) is the plural of delight/pleasantness. At God’s right hand, there are not just pleasures but pleasures upon pleasures — an inexhaustible supply.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "your faithful one" (hasîd) is David, but supremely Christ. David’s body decayed; Christ’s did not. The psalm transcends its original speaker. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: this is the Psalter’s most significant resurrection text. The NT apostles treated it as foundational proof of Christ’s bodily resurrection. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "eternal pleasures at your right hand" — the "right hand" is the place of honour and power. Eternal pleasure is not idle enjoyment but dignified participation in God’s presence. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David’s body rests secure (v.9), his soul will not be abandoned to Sheol (v.10), his flesh will not decay (v.10). God will show him the path of life — fullness of joy, eternal pleasures at God’s right hand (v.11). Peter’s Pentecost sermon (Acts 2) and Paul’s Antioch sermon (Acts 13) both identify this as Christ’s resurrection prophecy: David’s body decayed, but the one David prophesied about did not.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David claims the Levitical privilege: God himself is enough…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: 1), declares that apart from God he has no good (v…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI say of the holy people who are in the land, “They are the noble ones in whom is all my delight.”ESVAs for the saints in the land, they are the excellent ones, in whom is all my delight.NIVI keep my eyes always on the Lord. With him at my right hand, I will not be shaken.ESVI have set the LORD always before me; because he is at my right hand, I shall not be shaken.

rec.text: ""Apart from you I have no good thing." Total dependence on God as the source of all good."

rows.label: The Portion — 16:1–6
rows.text: "Apart from you I have no good thing." God as inheritance, portion, cup, lot.

hebtext.note: A miktam of David. The term appears in six psalm titles (16, 56–60). Its meaning is uncertain — possibly "inscription" or "golden poem." Psalm 16 is the most important because of its resurrection content.

hebtext.gloss: Sheol / the grave / the realm of the dead
hebtext.note: "You will not abandon me to the realm of the dead (shəʾōl), nor will you let your faithful one see decay" (v.10). The resurrection verse. Peter quotes this at Pentecost (Acts 2:25–28): David’s body DID see decay, so the psalm prophesies Christ’s resurrection. Paul repeats the argument (Acts 13:35–37). This is one of the OT’s clearest resurrection texts.

"You make known to me the path (nətîb) of life" (v.11). The "path of life" leads through death to resurrection. Not avoidance of death but passage through it. "You will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand." Joy, presence, eternity — the psalm’s final three words describe heaven.

thread.text: "I consider everything a loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ" — Paul’s version of "apart from you I have no good thing."
"The LORD is my portion; therefore I will wait for him" — the identical ḥēleq theology.
Peter quotes Psalm 16:8–11 at Pentecost: "You will not let your Holy One see decay" — applied to Christ’s resurrection.
Paul quotes Psalm 16:10 in Antioch: David died and decayed; Jesus did not. The psalm is prophetic.
"Death has been swallowed up in victory" — the resurrection the psalm anticipates.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the clearest OT resurrection text, quoted by Peter and Paul for Christ. Faith: God as sole portion. Worship: eternal joy in God’s presence.
